item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as no source found indicates it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoy the concept of a bird altering the weather around it to its own personal preferences, with no intended malice. I'll echo the remarks of thedeadlymoose above, in that the immediate decision to begin human sacrifice does seem a bit… too sudden, and jarring. I'd have thought a small town in the US would be more likely to follow Biblical tradition and try votive offerings of grain or berries (something a blackbird would actually be interested in) before possibly progressing to animal sacrifices.

Perhaps an unusually elongated El Nino event resulted in a shift from simple grain offerings to an offering of blood? Of course, the interview log's scope doesn't preclude the possibility that avenues other than human sacrifice were originally explored.

On a more minor note, should the containment procedures list the specific name(s) of individuals? What if they're promoted/killed/retired?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the way this pretty much harmless SCP has caused people to fall into bad pattern spotting, resulting in a body count :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the fact that they're able to scientifically prove the lack of necessity for human sacrifice. :D

item.title: Staff Post - Reversion Notice
item.content:encoded: Version 15 of this article has been reverted, as it made unnecessary minor changes without author permission.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like birds, but this simply wasn't interesting. The hook wasn't delivered very well through the dialogue, and the concept didn't seem to correspond with it well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm inclined to agree with moose.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep. This my second work ever on the site, and another rewrite candidate. I already did a bit of work on it, but I'm considering changing the recovery story to something a bit more unique.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's just alright. I don't like that the human sacrifice thing was played so incredibly straight. I've seen that cliche before. Can't we get a more original take on the idea?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you. This might get a rewrite in the near future, mostly to reduce the cumbersomeness of some of the passages.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really think this SCP is underrated for how cool it is. I love the way humans have reacted to it. It definitely needs more love. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A simple creature just trying to survive, with crazy humans doing their crazy human things. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a constant thing. The only time any considerable change is noticeable is during the El Nino (once again, much better than my original leap year idea, so thanks to pauli for that).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, but I have a question: does the bird conciously create the storms, is it just a constant thing, or does it just happen out of nowhere?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: that just reminds me too much of normal bird migration, up here in Canada we see the geese flying back when it starts getting warm again (I even use robins to let me know that my birthday is near) but I don't think we'd ever come to think that the geese are changing the weather.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it makes as much sense as anything; superstitions form very easily. Perhaps there was once a flock of birds that came and went with the good weather; now there is only this last survivor, and the townsfolk have been raised for generations to help it stay, with blood if needed. I admit I was much more interested in the history of the townsfolk sacrificing to their bird god than the actual effects of the creature.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ehhh, I still don't see how, but it's a good article anyways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: it was the only one chillin' in a hammock.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because of the agents snooping around. Everything the bird does is more noticeable the closer you are to it; years of living in the place and noticing its warmer and nicer the closest you get to the bird should be enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That still doesn't make much sense, they somehow knew it was that specific bird that was changing the temperature? Does it glow when it changes the temperature or does it become super hot? Why did the town even have meetings about their temperature shifting bird?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Edited the article according to what vezaz suggested.
As for how they knew it was the bird, the locals had a long time to watch it do its thing (the temperature change is strongest near it) and the agents overheard their meeting about the bird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is neat, but how did anyone pinpoint the phenomenon to a single bird?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I suppose it does look a tad awkward. I'll try to edit it to flow more naturally.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't we have a butterfly that does the same thing? I might be wrong, just wondering.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's language encumberences that are holding this SCP back.

"Tests on SCP-1948 found no relation between its weather control abilities and the townsfolk's increased hostility, nor was any connection found between them and the ritualistic sacrifices the townsfolk made to SCP-1948, contrary to their beliefs." is very awkward. You should break it into two declarative sentences, one about the hostility vs weather, and one about the sacrifices vs weather. Similar rewrites would make this SCP better. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The flow here killed it for me. This is a sorta-neat idea, but the execution doesn't work for me. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I changed the weather change to correspond with the El Niño and removed "Interesting".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's actually a really good idea. I think I'll change it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Addendum SCP-1948-1: Further examination of SCP-1948's weather changes in captivity found an interesting pattern: every leap year the specimen's preferred temperature would drop from twenty seven (27) degrees centigrade to five (5) degrees centigrade for a period of three months. This correlated to the time of the human sacrifice practiced by the residents of ███████ █████, though the pattern continued regularly without any need for the sacrifice practiced by the locals.

a: "interesting" is non-clinical, and adds nothing.

b: this would be much better if tied to something like el nino or sun spot cycles, rather than leap years.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The concept doesn't hook me in, though I like the whole 'raid on Innsmouth' vibe of its recovery. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this. A rather harmless SCP, but one that seemingly warped the people around it indirectly.
